Title: Engineering linear, branched-chain triterpene metabolism in monocots

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Panicum virgatum UBIQUITIN2 promoter with 5′ UTR and intron 1 (constitutive)
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutive transgene expression in engineered lines
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables non-native botryococcene accumulation in monocot leaves; cytosolic BS alone gives modest accumulation, plastid-targeted BS alone gives low accumulation without visible growth penalty
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cytosolic expression of BS in Brachypodium distachyon produced botryococcene at modest levels (~40 µg/g FW in T0 plants; highest ~128 ± 9 µg/g FW). 2. In Sorghum bicolor, cytosolic BS alone also supported botryococcene accumulation, reaching 26 ± 10 µg/g FW in tested lines. 3. Plastid-targeted BS alone in B. distachyon caused no abnormal phenotype but did not yield substantial botryococcene.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transformation; GC-MS metabolite profiling; 1H-NMR verification of purified botryococcene; crude leaf enzyme assay
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plant age/developmental stage increased accumulation; mature and senescing leaves accumulated more botryococcene; Sorghum grown under short autumn greenhouse days
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No visible detrimental phenotype when BS alone was targeted to cytosol or plastid
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BS was a primary experimental gene for introducing non-native triterpene biosynthesis. In yeast studies, BSm also produced a small amount of squalene (<10% relative catalysis for botryococcene), sufficient to modestly restore sterol prototrophy when combined with SQE genes.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful entry gene for engineering non-native triterpene hydrocarbons in monocots; best combined with FPS and cytosolic targeting for higher productivity

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endogenous in B. distachyon; heterologously expressed in yeast
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves of cytosolic BS + FPS Brachypodium lines; yeast cells coexpressing BSm + BdSQE1
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes formation of oxidobotryococcene from botryococcene, revealing endogenous oxidation of the engineered triterpene product
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A new compound proportional to botryococcene accumulation in cytosolic BS + FPS Brachypodium lines was inferred to be oxidobotryococcene. 2. The endogenous B. distachyon gene BRADI1G69170 was identified as the only clear SQE homolog and cloned as BdSQE1. 3. Coexpression of BSm and BdSQE1 in an SQE/SQS-deficient yeast strain produced a new peak with mass spectrum matching the in planta oxidobotryococcene.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; heterologous yeast complementation; GC-MS mass spectral matching; sterol prototrophy restoration assays
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Detected specifically in cytosolic botryococcene-producing context with high botryococcene accumulation
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May divert engineered botryococcene into oxidized side-products
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The oxidized product could represent 2,3-oxidobotryococcene and/or 20,21-oxidobotryococcene; co-elution of isoforms was considered possible. The paper frames BdSQE1 mainly as the endogenous modifying enzyme for the engineered product, not as the primary pathway-engineering target.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important caveat for engineering botryococcene accumulation because endogenous SQE promiscuity can create oxidized derivatives; may need suppression/editing if pure hydrocarbon accumulation is desired

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Zea mays UBIQUITIN1 promoter with 5′ UTR and intron 1 (constitutive)
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutive transgene expression in engineered lines
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: FPP precursor supply to botryococcene/sterol branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses dimethylallyl diphosphate with two molecules of isopentenyl diphosphate to form farnesyl diphosphate
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established as rate-limiting in this paper; FPP supply was inferred to limit cytosolic botryococcene accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Coexpression with BS strongly increased cytosolic botryococcene accumulation in Brachypodium, but plastid-targeted FPS caused chlorosis, stunting, premature senescence, and failure to set seed; in Sorghum, added FPS did not improve accumulation in the limited lines tested
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Cytosolic coexpression of FPS with BS in B. distachyon increased botryococcene from modest levels to >1 mg/g FW in high-producing mature lines, indicating FPP precursor supply limits accumulation. 2. Cytosolic BS + FPS lines remained phenotypically similar to wild type and did not significantly reduce total phytosterol content. 3. Plastid-targeted FPS was detrimental in B. distachyon, causing chlorosis and inability to survive on soil under phototrophic conditions. 4. In S. bicolor, cytosolic BS + FPS lines accumulated botryococcene, but not more than BS-only lines in the small sample tested; plastid-targeted tpBS + tpFPS yielded no detectable botryococcene.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transformation; subcellular targeting comparison; GC-MS metabolite profiling; phytosterol quantification; phenotype analysis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Subcellular compartment was decisive; plastid targeting caused severe deleterious effects in Brachypodium; accumulation increased with developmental age; Sorghum tested under short autumn greenhouse days
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Plastid-targeted FPS caused chlorotic, stunted plants with premature senescence and failure to set seed in Brachypodium; no clear gain in Sorghum under tested conditions
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The heterologous FPS source species was not specified in the provided text. Discussion suggests flux from IPP/DMAPP increased in BS + FPS lines without major phytosterol depletion, implying regulatory compensation in sterol biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RT-qPCR of selected endogenous MVA-pathway transcripts (AACT, HMGR1/HMGR2, SQS, IDI1/IDI2); no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Cytosolic FPS stacking with BS is an effective strategy to enhance botryococcene in monocots, but plastid targeting should be avoided in Brachypodium-like monocot systems

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endogenous in Brachypodium; heterologously expressed in yeast for validation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Epoxidizes the near-terminal double bond(s) of botryococcene to generate oxidobotryococcene; also supports squalene epoxidation in sterol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves of cytosolic BS + FPS Brachypodium lines; yeast cultures
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates oxidized derivative(s) of the engineered triterpene botryococcene, demonstrating endogenous side metabolism of the product
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BdSQE1 was identified as the sole clear SQE homolog in the B. distachyon genome using Arabidopsis SQE1 as query. 2. BdSQE1 restored sterol prototrophy in SQE/SQS-deficient yeast when paired with SQSfull, confirming SQE activity. 3. BdSQE1 coexpressed with BSm in yeast generated a peak whose mass spectrum matched the oxidobotryococcene observed in transgenic plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene cloning; yeast complementation; growth rescue assays; GC-MS
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Product detected under high botryococcene accumulation in cytosolic-engineered lines
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Converts desired hydrocarbon product into oxidized side-product(s)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper suggests possible formation of both 2,3- and 20,21-oxidobotryococcene isomers due to asymmetry of botryococcene and SQE promiscuity.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for suppression/editing to reduce unwanted oxidation of botryococcene during metabolic engineering
